evals.prompt: Set up react-grab in my Next.js App Router project so I can copy component context to Claude Code
evals.expected_output: Add react-grab Script tag to app/layout.tsx with process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' guard, or run npx -y grab@latest init to auto-detect
assertions: Provides Next.js App Router specific installation instructions with Script tag
Shows NODE_ENV development guard to prevent production loading
Mentions npx -y grab@latest init as the recommended auto-detect method

evals.prompt: Add react-grab to my Claude Code setup so I can feed element context directly to the agent
evals.expected_output: Run npx -y grab@latest add claude-code or bash scripts/add-agent.sh claude-code
assertions: Explains the workflow: hover element → press Cmd+C → paste into Claude Code

evals.prompt: How do I use react-grab to grab a React component's file path and copy it to my AI agent?
evals.expected_output: Hover over element in browser, press Cmd+C (Mac) or Ctrl+C (Windows/Linux), clipboard contains component name + file path + HTML
assertions: Shows example clipboard output format with component name and file path
Describes that output is ready to paste into AI agent

evals.prompt: Create a custom react-grab plugin that adds a 'Create Jira Ticket' action when I right-click an element
evals.expected_output: Use registerPlugin with actions array containing ContextMenuAction with onAction handler
assertions: Creates ContextMenuAction with id, label, shortcut, and onAction
Shows access to context.filePath and context.componentName in action handler

evals.prompt: Install the react-grab MCP server so my AI agent can programmatically access element context
evals.expected_output: Run npx -y grab@latest add mcp to install @react-grab/mcp server, then agent calls get_element_context tool
assertions: Explains get_element_context MCP tool that returns element context
Notes that user must select element in browser before agent can call the tool
